item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This one just doesn't click with me. I'm honestly not entirely sure why. It could be the slightly jarring shift from peaceful explorers to violent mob or perhaps the lack of containment shown. The author stated here that the Foundation would have to burn down the forest to find them and that's exactly what I expect the Foundation would do. In my mind the Foundation would happily raze a few kilometres of forest to better contain a dangerous and poorly understood scip. It's a good enough entry to keep me from down voting but not interesting enough for me to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Let me put it this way; If this was just this scip that bends light and is so deadly when angry, it would be a downvote from me.
What saved it in my eyes is the description, the report and that conclusion it contained. Built some tension, made a reveal, but at the same time avoided making it too dangerous in some ridiculus way.
While not the best thing I've ever seen here, it's well written and enjoyable to read.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sort of like what WE'RE doing to Mars.

item.title: Image Restoration
item.content:encoded: Upon receiving permission from the owner, this image has been restored with a watermark, and a link to their site.

item.title: Image Removal
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1320's image was removed because we could not properly determine whether or not the image was properly licensed or attributed. It may be added once again if/when proper attribution or proper use under CC guidelines is confirmed. Please contact Fantem or another member of the Image Team if you have questions.

Source:
http://www.robmulholland.co.uk/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To be honest… That's basically what's going on =P They're keeping the things so focused on new sources of stimulus that they don't even realize there's a whole world out there to explore. There's also not much coordination to their hostility beyond "Someone's hurting us KILL KILL KILL KILL". The security forces end up having the same responses, and it escalates into massive running gun-battles against half seen things.

When they're not in defense mode, though, they're earnestly just curious. And, well, it's not *their* fault they don't understand that these things that are moving around but exhibit no signs of being any sort of life form they recognize actually ARE alive and sentient. I always envisioned the buggers as being exceptionally smart but *exceedingly* narrow-minded, hence why they can be so easily distracted, have no real middle ground between peaceful exploration and maimkillburn, don't take into account that "this thing looks interesting, let me carve it down to the molecular level to see how it works" isn't always a good long-term strategy, etc. And, like I said, "These things are acting like they're alive. But they're not like life as we know it, therefore they're not REALLY alive."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the general idea, but it feels… hmm. It feels like the super-monster aspect is crowding out the alien explorer aspect. An incidentally dangerous thing, a thing that's dangerous because it is curious rather than hostile, that is more interesting to me than a monster that's mad and wants to get you. And their coordinated hostility undermines their curiosity, even if they're being defensive.

I think this would be much more interesting if the entire angle came out to be that the Foundation had to pretty much hide itself and its personnel and the public from these things by giving them something else to examine because all previous attempts to contain or examine the beings lead to them getting dangerously curious about humanity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This scip needs more attention.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What are you talking about? The concrete wall is and always has been 4 meters high and there has never been a typo stating 3 meters. Not once. And it certainly wasn't just now fixed so long after original posting.

In all seriousness though, I'm not sure about whether or not "native" is the proper word, but "originating" certainly isn't either. I basically went with the best I could come up with. And the wall isn't the primary means of containment: keeping them entertained with new and various items to study is. The wall is there to A) make sure they don't accidentally wander into some new spot and thus expand their "viable" area, and B) to slow them down enough if they DO start trying to get through that a response team can be mobilized to deal with it somehow.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This SCP really grabbed me, it has the feel of something that is actively developing. Most SCPs tend to become static once contained, but this one has some real potential to grow.

A couple of points though, SCP-1320 is said to be native to the containment area. How was this concluded? How was the Foundation made aware of them in the first place? Also, is a concrete wall that is shorter than some of them really effective containment. even when combined with the detectors, it would seem that if there was any desire on their part to leave it would be no more useful than a smooth top 1.8m chain link fence for humans. even if made taller, what would prevent them from climbing it Wolverine style?
In any case, take my +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like how these creatures function. Generally peaceful creatures that can be a threat when provoked. That being said I feel that the super speed kind of takes away from them. Tbe articles hook would be better if say the entities habitat had already expanded farther than previously though and that's why the jeep was destroyed after moving so far from the initial area.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think it was a nice touch to make them relatively peaceful, until one of them was attacked. Too many SCPs seem to have it out for humanity just because we exist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, that's basically it. Any time someone tries to grab or lead them anywhere, zerg rush. If they could have herded them into a proper site, they would have. It's the fact that they'd need to firebomb the entire forest into ash to be able to actually find and grab them all. They're making sure that they don't go further than they have because, once their "known territory" goes further, they just start appearing in the new areas. So until they're ABLE to properly contain and control them, they're basically making sure these guys don't start wandering around looking for new shit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's no real connection between their odd appearance, their choice of habitat, and their behavior. Since they have the potential to increase their area of influence I question why they haven't just been rounded up and kept in a site by now. They're deadly, but no more than many other SCPs currently locked away.

I think there's potential in intensely curious creatures as an SCP, but I think you could come up with a better reason for continually providing them with new objects than "They'll kill people otherwise."

Withholding vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the revisions you made, and I think that it just needed a little time to settle with me. Upvote!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted for potential, and for a D-class who triggered a massacre by throwing a rock at an SCP's head.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've touched up a bit of the grammar, wording and all. I've also gotten rid of the "ongoing" feel of the crossed out sections and "upgrade pending" note. If anyone spots anything I've missed, please feel free to go ahead and take care of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I was kind of going for the "in transition" feel, but having been looking at it in my sandbox for a while, and seeing it up here, I'm getting rid of that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like your explanation of the pseudo-invisibility, as well as how you address the extreme weirdness of their being able to perceive anything or communicate. And I like the "lethal examination" aspect. There's just something about this that isn't grabbing me as a whole, though, and I can't figure out what. There's nothing bad per se about this, but it's just not doing it for me.

Upvote, but only a tentative one, for the reasons above.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fair enough. The "lethally curious" and "sharp claws" both basically came from the same base fact that they're stupefyingly thin for an apparently complex creature of their size. Things that are so thin tend to be either one of two things: incredibly brittle/flimsy, or incredibly sharp. And yes I said "tend to", I know there's plenty of other possible combinations ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I did, pulled it up in chat a couple times, got some feedback. One of my main problems is that I tend to pick… Odd ways of wording things, and when trying to make stuff flow better I either miss a spot or end up fixing half a sentence but not the other half. I'll look over this again when I get some free time later, but I always appreciate anyone who's willing to fix any sort of spelling/grammatical problems I miss.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted getting you to minus two. I know I'm odd here but I really like these guys just the way they are.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't care for hate dislike articles that cross out strike through old data instead of just deleting revising replacing it with the new relevant data.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This just confuses me, and lacks a hook :/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This isn't bad, but it just doesn't sit right with me. I think the problem with it is that it's just sort of a mishmash of interesting concepts that don't really gel. They're invisible-ish, they're lethally curious, they've got incredibly sharp claws; they're all interesting attributes that aren't naturally cohesive to one another. There's a couple tone errors sprinkled in there too (calling them "stealthy" seems sort of off, and the second paragraph seems sort of awkwardly constructed), but they're nothing too major.

Overall…I dunno. Like I said, there's nothing really that wrong with it; it probably just comes down to personal tastes on my part.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This didn't really catch my attention, and I can't quite pick out why. There are a lot of small grammatical errors that bother me a great deal. Also, the section detailing that "At least once per week, 10 items should be introduced" does not really fit in special containment. Did you sandbox this idea or anything?
